query: role of ADRB gene polymorphisms in transient tachypnea of the newborn

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Infant Death Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Underlying Vulnerability
retrieved_docs.content: Infants dying as a result of SIDS display suboptimal physiologic regulatory responses. Post-mortem examination of the brainstems of SIDS victims demonstrates abnormalities in serotonergic signaling abnormalities in the arcuate nucleus and tissues that modulate ventilation and blood pressure in response to hypoxia and hypercarbia. [19] [20] Affected infants also show decreased 5-HT 1A receptor binding in the medulla, which influences a broad range of autonomic responses via serotonin signaling. [21] Some authors have identified gene polymorphisms that may predispose to SIDS. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Abstinence Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic mechanisms for differences in NAS expression have additionally been suggested. Single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNP) in the genes for opioid receptors (mu - OPRM1, delta - OPRD1, and kappa - OPRK1) and dopamine metabolism pathway (catechol-O-methyltransferase [COMT] gene) are associated with shorter hospital stay and duration of treatment. Studies evaluating SNPs in the genes for opioid metabolism, like the ABCB1 gene and cytochrome P450 (CYP) genes like CYP2B6, CYP2D6, and CYP34A, have been inconclusive. Besides the genetic mechanisms, epigenetics, such as DNA methylation, resulting in decreased expression of OPRM1 genes, has also correlated with increased NAS expression severity. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Medications do not play a routine role in the treatment of TTN, as it is a self-limited condition that typically resolves with supportive care alone within 72 hours. Inhaled beta-agonists, including salbutamol and albuterol, have been investigated for their potential to enhance lung fluid clearance by stimulating sodium reabsorption through ENaC channels. [26] Salbutamol administration may slightly decrease the duration of symptoms and hospital stay, but further clinical trials are needed to confirm its efficacy and safety. [27] [28] Current evidence does not support the routine use of diuretics, racemic epinephrine, or postnatal corticosteroids in the management of TTN. [29] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cesarean delivery, particularly when performed before the onset of labor, is a well-established predisposing factor for TTN. The risk is primarily attributed to the absence of hormonal and physiological changes associated with labor, most notably a catecholamine surge, which facilitates the absorption of alveolar fluid into the lymphatic and vascular systems. [13] Although thoracic compression during vaginal delivery may also aid in expelling some alveolar fluid, this mechanism likely plays a secondary role compared to hormonally driven clearance. Reflecting these concerns, the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) issued guidelines in 2013 advising against nonmedically indicated cesarean deliveries before 39 weeks' gestation, in part to reduce respiratory morbidity, including TTN. [14] Other risk factors for TTN include maternal obesity, maternal diabetes, fetal male sex, and being small or large for gestational age. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiopulmonary Arrest in Adults -- Etiology -- Inherited Disorders
retrieved_docs.content: Early repolarization syndrome. Brugada syndrome Short QT syndrome. Long QT syndrome. Catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia

retrieved_docs.title: Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Addressing risk factors associated with TTN may help prevent its occurrence. One initiative is the ACOG guideline against performing non-medically indicated cesarean deliveries before 39 weeks' gestation. Prenatal administration of betamethasone to women at risk of late preterm delivery has shown promise in reducing the incidence and severity of TTN. This benefit is thought to occur by upregulation of ENAc, which is involved in lung fluid absorption. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Tocolysis -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Beta-Adrenergic receptor agonists specifically work on the beta-2 receptor. Activation of the beta-2 receptors causes an increase in cyclic AMP (cAMP) which is associated with increased smooth muscle relaxation. The most common medication in this class is terbutaline. Hexoprenaline, a beta-2 receptor agonist, is used in many countries but has yet to be approved by the US Food and Drug Administration. Ritodrine is also another medication in the category that is used internationally for tocolysis but is not approved in the United States. There has been a black box warning issued by the US Food and Drug Administration against the use of injectable terbutaline in the setting of prolonged preterm labor-management (over 72 hours) due to maternal cardiac complications. These complications are suggested from terbutaline's activity at beta-1 receptors, which are located in cardiac muscle. Studies have suggested the tocolytic agents in this family might confer a risk of the development of childhood asthma. [14] Maternal risks include but are not limited to cardiac arrhythmias, tachycardia, hypotension, nausea, and vomiting. Fetal risks include but are not limited to tachycardia.

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Death in Athletes -- Prognosis -- Catecholaminergic Polymorphic Ventricular Tachycardia
retrieved_docs.content: Poor prognosis if it is not diagnosed early as it often presents in childhood. ICDs and beta-blockers drastically improve outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Delayed clearance of fetal lung fluid after birth is the underlying cause of TTN. Residual fluid within the alveoli and interstitium impairs gas exchange, resulting in tachypnea and respiratory distress. Normally, this fluid is cleared through a physiologic shift in the pulmonary epithelium from chloride secretion to active sodium reabsorption, a process stimulated by the release of catecholamines during labor. Well-established maternal risk factors for TTN are gestational diabetes, asthma, and cesarean delivery without labor. [5] [6] Fetal and neonatal risk factors include gestational age younger than 39 weeks, male sex, small or large size for gestational age, and perinatal asphyxia. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Sodium Nitroprusside -- Contraindications -- Nitroprusside is Contraindicated in the Following Situations
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of compensatory hypertension, such as that seen with arteriovenous malformations or aortic coarctation SNP should not be used for inducing controlled perioperative hypotension in patients with known inadequate cerebral perfusion Acute heart failure with low systemic vascular resistance (e.g., septic shock) Leber's hereditary optic atrophy* Tobacco amblyopia* Vitamin B12 deficiency (or associated disease states) SNP must not be used simultaneously with phosphodiesterase inhibitors such as sildenafil, tadalafil, or vardenafil (due to the synergistic cGMP-mediated mechanism of action) Known hypersensitivity to nitroprusside or any component of the formulation

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Neonatal -- Clinical Significance -- Pearls
retrieved_docs.content: Neonates possess a dominant parasympathetic tone placing them at risk for bradycardic episodes under stressful circumstances.

retrieved_docs.title: Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: This article reviews the epidemiology, risk factors, and pathophysiology of TTN to help clinicians understand its underlying mechanisms and natural course. Practical guidance on diagnosis and management, including how to differentiate TTN from more serious causes of neonatal respiratory distress and when to escalate the level of care, is also offered. Although TTN is typically self-limited, the priority is to exclude more critical respiratory conditions. With early diagnosis and evidence-based management, clinicians can minimize unnecessary interventions while ensuring safe and effective care for affected neonates.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Etiology -- Genetics
retrieved_docs.content: Monozygotic twins have a higher incidence of RDS compared to dizygotic twins, and an increased incidence of RDS has also been reported in families, thus supporting an underlying genetic predisposition. [9] Infants with genetic causes of surfactant protein deficiency can also present with varying degrees of RDS. Rare recessive mutations of the SP-B gene causing SP-B deficiency can present in the neonatal period with severe RDS and progress to severe respiratory failure. [10] On the other hand, SP-C gene mutations are seen in about 0.1% of the population and present with interstitial lung disease, usually beyond the first month of life. Neonatal RDS is also associated with deletions in ATP binding cassette sub-family A, member 3 (ABCA-3). Although about 4% of the population reportedly carries this deletion, the exact incidence of fatal RDS in this population is unknown. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn presents with severe hypoxemia, a difference between preductal and postductal oxygen saturations, and may be associated with underlying parenchymal lung disease. Echocardiography is a diagnostic tool that confirms elevated pulmonary pressures and rules out structural heart disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Transient Tachypnea of the Newborn -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Pneumonia and sepsis present with respiratory distress, often with temperature instability, poor perfusion, or hypotension. A chest x-ray may show patchy infiltrates or consolidation. Positive blood cultures, abnormal complete blood count, and inflammatory markers support the diagnosis. [2]

retrieved_docs.content: Timolol, a non‑selective beta‑adrenergic blocker, should not be used in patients who have تَشَنُّج قَصَبِيّ (bronchial spasm or asthma) because the drug can worsen airway constriction and precipitate respiratory distress. Recognizing this contraindication is essential for safe prescribing and for preventing adverse respiratory events in individuals with underlying bronchospastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2051
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergní antagonisté refers to the class of adrenergic antagonists, a group of drugs that block the actions of catecholamines at adrenergic receptors. Within this class, fentolamin and oxidopamin are specific agents that are considered “children” of the broader adrenergic antagonist category, indicating that they share the core pharmacologic properties of receptor blockade. Profenamine is also classified as an adrenergic antagonist, further expanding the range of compounds that act through this mechanism. The use and effects of adrenergic antagonists are closely linked to متلازمة الأدرينالية (adrenal syndrome), which can serve as a causative factor influencing the clinical indication for these drugs or contributing to their adverse effect profile. Understanding the relationships among these agents and adrenal syndrome is important for appropriate therapeutic selection and management of patients requiring adrenergic blockade.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists, whose mechanism of action involves blocking β‑adrenergic receptors, have a contraindicated mechanism of action with methacholin, a muscarinic receptor agonist. Because the pharmacologic effects of β‑blockade oppose the cholinergic stimulation produced by methacholin—potentially leading to adverse outcomes such as enhanced bronchoconstriction or undesirable cardiovascular effects—these agents should not be used together in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1787
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergní antagonisté constitute a pharmacologic class of antiadrenergic agents that act by blocking adrenergic receptors, particularly alpha receptors. Within this class are several specific compounds, including antiadrenergic alpha receptor agents, dihydroergokristin mesylát, fentolamin, oxidopamin, and profenamine. Each of these agents is considered a child or subclass of the broader adrenergní antagonisté group, sharing the common mechanism of antagonizing adrenergic signaling and thereby influencing cardiovascular and other autonomic functions. Understanding the relationships among these agents helps clinicians select appropriate adrenergic‑blocking drugs for conditions such as hypertension, arrhythmias, and other disorders mediated by excessive adrenergic activity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2544
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergní antagonisté, a pharmacologic class of adrenergic antagonists, includes dihydroergokristin and its mesylate salt, dihydroergokristin mesylát, as members of the same group. In addition, fentolamin is identified as a child entity within the adrenergní antagonisté class, reflecting its role as a specific adrenergic‑blocking agent. All three compounds share the common mechanism of antagonizing adrenergic receptors, which underlies their therapeutic use in conditions where inhibition of sympathetic activity is desired. Recognizing dihydroergokristin, dihydroergokristin mesylát, and fentolamin as related members of the adrenergní antagonisté class helps clinicians understand their similar pharmacodynamic properties and informs appropriate selection and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2648
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergní látky, the class of adrenergic substances, encompasses specific compounds such as cyclopentamine and tuaminoheptane, which are regarded as child agents within this pharmacologic group. Both cyclopentamine and tuaminoheptane share the common characteristic of acting on adrenergic receptors, contributing to the overall effects associated with adrenergic stimulation. Recognizing these compounds as members of the adrenergní látky family helps clinicians understand their mechanism of action, potential therapeutic uses, and side‑effect profiles in the context of sympathomimetic drug therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1953
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic antagonists constitute a subclass of neurotransmitter antagonists that block the actions of catecholamines at adrenergic receptors. Within this pharmacologic group, compounds such as fentolamine and oxidopamine are identified as child agents, reflecting their specific roles as adrenergic‑blocking drugs. Additional agents associated with the class include dihydroergocristine and profenamine, which are also regarded as members of the adrenergic antagonist family. By inhibiting the effects of norepinephrine and epinephrine, these agents are used to modulate sympathetic activity in various therapeutic settings, underscoring the importance of understanding their relationships within the broader category of neurotransmitter antagonism.
